text: THE ASSESSMENT OF BLEEDING IN CHILDREN Management Guidelines Emergency Department Princess Margaret Hospital for Children Perth, Western Australia Last reviewed: October 2002 Page 1 of 1 Dr Gary Geelhoed Dr James Price The Assessment of Bleeding in Children DPMS No 3474 Page 1 Common presentations of bleeding disorders are: (a) Excessive bruising and bleeding after trauma (b) Recurrent epistaxis and mucosal bleeding (c) Bleeding after operations and dental extractions (d) Spontaneous haemarthroses only in severe factor deficiency History and Physical Examination: Try and determine whether this is an acquired or inherited bleeding problem, and whether this is a platelet disorder or a coagulation deficiency. How long have symptoms been present, and is the process local or general. Platelet problems usually present with mucosal and skin bleeding whereas coagulation defects present with deep muscle haematomas, haemarthroses, but also have skin bruising. Be alert to the possibility of non-accidental injury, bleeding disorder associated with hepatic and renal disease and, rarely, connective tissue problems. Investigations: (a) Full blood count, including platelets and blood film (b) Standard coagulation profile, including prothrombin time, APPT, thrombin clotting time and fibrinogen. (c) Platelet function studies assessed with PFA100. This is used as a screening test for platelet function abnormalities and for von Willebrand’s disease (VWD). If there is a family history of VWD or a strong suspicion clinically, factor VIII studies including Ristocetin cofactor and von Willebrand factor antigen should be performed. Aggregation studies are performed if there is a family history of platelet dysfunction or the PFA100 is abnormal. Drugs such as Aspirin are a common source of platelet dysfunction. Common Diagnoses: (See Haemophilia and von Willebrand’s Disease guideline for more details) (a) Haemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency): A sex linked condition but approximately 30% of new cases have no family history. Can be mild, moderate or severe depending on factor levels. Screening tests: prolonged APTT which corrects with normal plasma. All other tests normal. (b) Haemophilia B: (Factor IX deficiency): Similar to Haemophilia A with mild, moderate and severe cases. Screening tests: prolonged APTT which completely corrects with normal plasma. All other tests normal. (c) von Willebrand’s disease: A common mild bleeding disorder usually presenting with bruising and mucosal bleeding. Menorrhagia and post-partum haemorrhage are common problems in females. Inherited as an autosomal dominant in most cases. Most common form is type 1 where there is a quantitative deficiency, i.e. reduced factor VIII coagulant, Ristocetin cofactor, and von Willebrand factor antigen. In type 2 disorders there is a quantitative abnormality with reduced Ristocetin cofactor relative to a von Willebrand factor antigen. A rare form is the severe type 3 disorder, where patients are homozygotes and levels of factor VIII coagulant, Ristocetin cofactor, and von Willebrand factor antigen are all markedly reduced. (d) Lupus anticoagulant: Due to an antiphospholipid antibody. In children, usually a transient, post infection abnormality rarely clinically significant. Screening tests: Prolonged APTT which does not correct with normal plasma.
